data.title: Jealous Hongkonger who threatened to burn 6 media newsrooms jailed for 1 year
data.description:
A former Hong Kong civil servant has been jailed for 12 months for threatening to burn down the newsrooms of the South China Morning Post and five other media outlets if they refused to publish seditious material he had created to frame the husband of his former girlfriend.
West Kowloon Court on Tuesday heard the guilty pleas from Tse Chun-pan, 43, who had set up a website selling merchandise themed around the 2019 anti-government protests and demanded that six local media publish screenshots of a page containing the seditious content.
Tse, a former social security officer with the Social Welfare Department, admitted to listing his love rival’s phone number as the seller’s contact on the website.
The victim, whose identity was withheld, is married to a woman Tse briefly dated in 2015.
The court heard that between 2016 and 2018, the victim had been repeatedly targeted by an unknown person who posted his photo and harassed him on an online forum, and even filed a false report to the Independent Commission Against Corruption accusing him of fraudulently obtaining sick leave.
The victim, who even received paper funeral effigies after his child was born in 2018, believed Tse was behind the harassment.
Last June, six media outlets – including the SCMP, The Standard and China Daily – received a threatening letter warning them to comply with the requests to publish the screenshots.
The letter stated: “I warn you all, you must publish this for me for free, otherwise I will burn your newsroom down.”
Six staff members separately made reports to the police.
The victim was taken to a police station for investigation where he told officers about Tse’s “shenanigans” over the years.
Tse was arrested by police last July and later charged with knowingly publishing seditious articles and six counts of threatening to destroy or damage property.
He reached a plea bargain with the prosecution and pleaded guilty to four charges.
In mitigation, Tse said he had been suffering from a mental disorder and had “acted out of character.”
Mitigation letters from people who had previously benefited from Tse’s service praised him as an “extraordinary” civil servant who had gone “above and beyond” for the community.
The defence counsel said the defendant had no intention of endangering national security or committing arson, but had acted out of revenge against the victim.
Chief Magistrate So Wai-tak said the defendant had premeditated framing the victim and harassing him for years before the present case.
“The defendant’s current predicament is the very result of what he initially intended to inflict on [the victim]. He reaps what he sows,” he said in sentencing.

data.title: Singapore’s Lawrence Wong to visit Hong Kong after Boao Forum trip
data.description:
Singapore’s Prime Minister Lawrence Wong will arrive in Hong Kong on Thursday for a three-day visit, meeting the city’s leader, politicians and businesspeople, and will also examine key sites in the Northern Metropolis megaproject to explore collaboration opportunities.
It will be Wong’s first official visit to Hong Kong since he assumed office as Singapore’s fourth prime minister in May 2024.
The visit will follow Wong’s attendance at the Boao Forum for Asia annual conference in Hainan province, where he will deliver a keynote speech on Wednesday.
Wong is scheduled to meet Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu at Government House on Friday, according to the Hong Kong government. The two leaders would meet over lunch hosted by Lee, the Prime Minister’s Office in Singapore said.
“Prime Minister Wong will visit key sites in the Northern Metropolis to gain a better understanding of Hong Kong’s economic and development trajectory and explore new opportunities for collaboration between our two cities,” the office said.
It added that Wong, who is also the country’s finance minister, would meet Hong Kong political and business leaders, and engage with Singaporeans in the city at a reception.

Wong previously visited Hong Kong in May 2019, as minister for national development and second minister for finance, sharing insights on Singapore’s urban planning with local audiences. He also met then chief executive Carrie Lam Cheng Yuet-ngor.
He first met Lee in July 2023 during the Hong Kong leader’s visit to Singapore, when Wong was serving as deputy prime minister.
During Wong’s stay in Hainan this week, he will also attend a welcome dinner hosted by the provincial government and have bilateral meetings with National People’s Congress chairman Zhao Leji and Feng Fei, Communist Party secretary for the Hainan provincial committee.
He will be accompanied by Senior Minister of State in the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Ministry of Home Affairs Sim Ann and Senior Minister of State in the Ministry of Trade and Industry and the Ministry of Culture, Community and Youth Low Yen Ling, among other key officials.

data.title: Alibaba debuts what it calls most powerful CPU core of its kind
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding’s research arm Damo Academy has unveiled a chip designed to fuel artificial intelligence agents, as the tech giant bets on open-source RISC-V architecture to ride the wave of agentic AI.
XuanTie C950, the latest flagship in Alibaba’s XuanTie RISC-V series, was introduced at the company’s annual ecosystem conference in Shanghai on Tuesday. Designed for high-performance tasks in cloud computing and AI computing, the C950 is a “CPU core” – the fundamental architecture for a chip – that Alibaba claims to be the most powerful of its kind globally.
Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Meng Jianyi, chief scientist at Damo Academy, said the C950 delivers more than three times the performance of the C920, a previous generation of the series which also targeted high-performance computing.
The launch comes as AI agents – programmes that are capable of autonomously performing tasks on behalf of a user or another system – are becoming the latest battlefield for Chinese tech companies, after the OpenAI-backed AI agent OpenClaw took the industry by storm. Tech giants like Alibaba, Tencent Holdings and ByteDance have rushed to launch their own versions or integrate the agent into their apps.
In technical terms, the new chip features an 8-instruction decode width and a 16-stage pipeline, which essentially means the chip can read and execute a vast number of commands.

Through co-optimised software and hardware, the C950 delivers leading performance for cloud computing tasks including MySQL, Redis, Nginx and OpenSSL. It can also power inferencing of large language models including Qwen and DeepSeek when used alongside the company’s self-developed AI computing engines – the Vector Acceleration Engine and the Matrix Acceleration Engine – which were also launched on Tuesday.
The C950 represents the latest move by the Hangzhou-based company to boost its capabilities in AI infrastructure and counter US chip restrictions by leveraging the open-standard architecture which is free of US export controls.
RISC-V, the fifth generation of the Reduced Instruction Set Computer, became available for chip developers to configure and customise under the Switzerland-based non-profit RISC-V International in 2015.
As an open-source project, it is free for anyone to use and modify, making it one of China’s best hopes for achieving self-sufficiency in chip design instead of relying on Intel’s x86 and Arm’s namesake architecture, which power the Microsoft Windows and Google Android operating systems, respectively.
The open-standard nature allowed chip designers to “customise instruction sets and accelerate specific AI workloads with no or low licensing fees”, Alibaba said. This was important for the development of AI agents as they were running across a vast spectrum of devices and consumed massive computing resources, it added.
Alibaba was an early mover in China’s RISC-V space, launching the XuanTie series in 2018. Chips in the series that have gained wide industry adoption include the C910 launched in 2019 and C920 in 2024.

The new chip followed the launch of the C930 at the same event a year ago, which marked the company’s first server-grade CPU. On Tuesday, the company also launched the C925, an optimised version of the C930 with higher energy efficiency.
“Alibaba is leading in China for RISC-V development and deployment,” Lu Dai, board chair of RISC-V International, said at the event. The architecture was now undergoing a shift from “purely technology development” to deployment, putting a greater focus on the ecosystem, he added.
Separately, T-Head, Alibaba’s chip design unit, has shipped more than 470,000 AI chips as of February and was nearing 10 billion yuan (US$1.45 billion) in annual revenue over the past two years, the company said during its earnings call last week.

data.title: Swedish businessman in Hong Kong loses appeal in domestic helper rape case
data.description:
A Swedish businessman convicted of raping his domestic helper has failed to clear his name and has been sent back to prison after a Hong Kong court dismissed his appeal.
The Court of Appeal on Tuesday upheld the conviction of Patrik Tobias Ekstrom, who was unanimously found guilty by a jury two years ago and sentenced to seven years in prison for raping his helper in his bedroom on October 27, 2022.
The Swedish national, in his late thirties, who was granted bail pending appeal on one count of rape and one of buggery without consent, maintained that the acts were consensual and the helper had entered his bedroom willingly.
He denied threatening to kill her if she did not comply with his demand for sex.
A three-judge panel had considered appeal arguments from Ekstrom’s counsel Elizabeth Herbert the trial judge had failed to give appropriate directions to the jury regarding evidence that might suggest Ekstrom had raped another helper in the past.
According to the victim’s testimony, when Ekstrom pressed her for sex, he told her he had previously had sex with the family’s former helper.
She said Ekstrom told her he had “done such a thing” before and could “get away with it again”.
Herbert argued that the allegations that Ekstrom raped the previous helper were supported only by the victim’s words and were not part of the charges in the present proceedings.
However, the lawyer said that mentioning the alleged offence could amount to “propensity evidence”, potentially leading the jury to believe that her client had committed other acts of sexual misconduct in the past.

The judges noted that the prosecutor at trial even intended to treat the evidence regarding history between the former helper and Ekstrom as irrelevant, but the defence deliberately raised the previous rape allegations as a tactic to discredit the victim.
The appellate court said the trial judge gave clear directions to the jury to deliberate whether the intercourse was consensual, which was the central issue at trial.
The previous rape allegations were not central to the trial as a whole. Therefore the absence of directions regarding such matters “did not give rise to real risk” that the jury’s verdict was improper, the court said.
The jury was not invited to draw any inference from the previous rape allegations about Ekstrom’s character or whether he was a repeat offender.
“Considering the trial as a whole, including the detailed and balanced directions the judge gave on the central issues of consent and credibility, we are not persuaded that the absence of a specific uncharged-act direction in relation to the [the previous helper] matter rendered the convictions unsafe,” the judges said.
The court concluded that the trial judge did not err in law and dismissed the appeal.
Ekstrom received the judgment in the dock and was taken back to prison to serve the remainder of his jail term after his bail was revoked.

data.title: Memory costs ‘beyond imagination’ hurting smartphone makers: Xiaomi leader
data.description:
Rising memory-chip costs could reshape the smartphone industry, said Xiaomi president William Lu Weibing, after weak smartphone sales cut into the firm’s fourth-quarter profit.
Memory prices were surging at a magnitude “beyond imagination”, drawing smartphone makers into a long-term price-raising cycle, Lu said on an earnings call on Tuesday.
“When the price-raising cycle ends, some players may suffer significant losses or even face closure,” he said, without elaborating. However, the challenge would also force the industry to make innovations, he added.
Xiaomi reported that fourth-quarter net profit dropped 27 per cent from a year earlier to 6.5 billion yuan (US$943 million), the lowest quarterly number in a year and the first year-on-year decline since the second quarter of 2024. Fourth-quarter revenue rose 7.3 per cent from a year earlier and 3 per cent from previous quarter to 116.9 billion yuan.
The results met analyst estimates of 5.78 billion yuan in profit and 116 billion yuan in sales.
Xiaomi felt the squeeze from the rising memory costs, as smartphone shipments in the fourth quarter dropped 11.6 per cent year on year to 37.7 million units, dragging revenue for the firm’s largest revenue contributor down 13.6 per cent to 44.3 billion yuan.
The average smartphone selling price sank 2.2 per cent to 1,176 yuan from 1,202 yuan a year ago despite the firm’s shift to premium models and the higher memory costs.
Morgan Stanley on Monday cut its forecast for global smartphone shipments this year by 15 per cent to 1.1 billion units, as it expected phone makers to pass most of the surging memory-chip costs on to consumers.
Rising costs also pushed Xiaomi to raise the selling price of its second-generation SU7 electric vehicle (EV), released in mid-March. Though the 4,000 yuan increase was less than expected, some analysts warned that the pricing strategy could be short-lived given weakening demand owing to the halving of a sales tax incentive in mainland China.
Xiaomi’s fourth-quarter revenue from EVs and other new initiatives – its second-largest income source since the third quarter of last year – surged 123.4 per cent year on year to 37.2 billion yuan.

However, Xiaomi’s EV delivery growth cooled significantly in late 2025, dropping from a 300 per cent peak in April to double digits by December. The slowdown followed a naturally high initial growth phase and was further compounded by fatal accidents involving its models that damaged consumer confidence.
“Though the memory costs and the phasing out of government-funded purchase subsidies are bringing short-term pressures, Xiaomi will continue to pursue an innovation-led strategy to facilitate the adoption of artificial intelligence and overseas expansion,” Lu said on the earnings call.
For the full year, Xiaomi recorded 457 billion yuan in revenue and 41.57 billion yuan in net profit, up 25 per cent and 76.3 per cent, respectively, from 2024.
Xiaomi’s Hong Kong-listed shares gained 1.9 per cent in Hong Kong to HK$32.68 on Tuesday. The stock has lost nearly 19 per cent this year and is down 46.8 per cent from its all-time high of HK$61.45 in June 2025.
Meanwhile, Hesai Group – a key supplier of Xiaomi’s auto unit – swung to a net profit of 435.9 million yuan in 2025 from a net loss of 102 million yuan a year earlier, as rising demand for lidar (laser distance and ranging) units, which are used to sense distance in cars and robots, drove shipments up by 223 per cent for the year to 1.62 million units.

data.title: Visiting galleries may keep artworks in Hong Kong for months due to shipping chaos
data.description:
Overseas galleries exhibiting at Art Central in Hong Kong are considering keeping their artworks in the city for months after the fair ends amid rising shipping prices as a result of the US-Israeli war on Iran.
Ahead of the fair’s opening at the Central Harbourfront on Wednesday, gallerists told the South China Morning Post that fuel charges for shipping to Hong Kong had increased by as much as four times.
Art Central brings together more than 100 galleries from the city, mainland China, South Korea, Japan, Singapore, the United States, Britain, Spain and Israel, among other countries, and runs until Sunday.
Simon Vargas, founder and director of Miami-based Wolf & Nomad gallery, said he was considering keeping the artworks in the city until it hosted the Affordable Art Fair, which usually takes place in May.
Shipments were difficult this year, Vargas said, adding that one crate with a large work was only due to arrive on Wednesday, forcing him to adjust how he presented his booth.
“We normally send through Qatar, but it cancelled and had the work sent back. Then we shipped it through Amsterdam, which was cancelled as well. We now had it shipped to New York first before coming to Hong Kong,” he said.

While carriers must refund payment if they cannot deliver a shipment, the extended times had prompted him to rethink his plan after the fair.
The manager of Dubai-based Rarares Gallery, Olga Prokhorova, said she had been worried whether there would be enough time to set up a booth as her artworks slowly made their way to the city.
The closure of airspace in the Middle East and the increase in fuel charges by as much as four times had complicated the process, she added. The pieces only arrived days ago, just ahead of Wednesday’s opening.
“I think costs will keep rising,” she said. “I don’t think we will [ship works back after the fair] for now. I would rather bring more art here to do exhibitions and projects in Hong Kong, instead of bringing them back, but I want to see enough demand, of course.
Prokhorova said the artworks could remain in Hong Kong for a few months, which would also give her time to consider her options ahead of Art Dubai in May.
Emma Guarnieri, sales director of Galleria Barovier&Toso from Venice, said she chose direct flights to ship glass sculptures to Hong Kong, avoiding the Middle Eastern routes that other Italian galleries usually favoured.
“It’s going to be very expensive to ship back,” she said, adding it was the gallery’s first venture into Asia. “We’re also thinking how we can activate the market … once you ship them here, it’s so expensive it’s worth doing at least two events.”
She added that keeping the sculptures in Asia would also make sense from a sales perspective, as clients might see a work at the fair and return only weeks later to buy it.

data.title: Cathay extends suspension of flights between Hong Kong, Middle East to end of May
data.description:
Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways has further extended its suspension of all flights to and from the Middle East until the end of May, amid a regional conflict that shows little sign of abating.
But the airline will increase flights to Europe starting next month to cater to surging demand.
The airline has extended its suspension of Middle East routes at least four times since early March. It announced just last week that services would be cancelled until the end of April.
In an announcement on Tuesday, Cathay said flights between Hong Kong and Dubai in the United Arab Emirates, as well as between the city and Riyadh in Saudi Arabia, would remain suspended until the end of May.
“We are monitoring the situation closely and will remain agile in our response,” the airline said. “The safety of our customers and people guides every decision we make.”
Affected travellers will be offered flexibility to rebook, reroute or cancel their tickets, Cathay said.
According to its website, travellers who have bought flights between Hong Kong and Riyadh up to June 30 can rebook, reroute or get refunds for their tickets without being charged the usual administrative fees.
The airline previously said it would waive rebooking, re-routing or refunding fees for passengers booked to travel between Hong Kong and the two Gulf cities up to May 31.

Meanwhile, in response to a surge in demand for flights to Europe, the airline said it would operate three additional return flights to Paris in France and Zurich in Switzerland next month, while adding seats on 13 return flights to the British capital, London.
An escalation in regional hostilities, triggered by reciprocal strikes between US-Israeli forces and Iran, has severely disrupted Middle Eastern airspace, leading to a massive wave of flight cancellations affecting millions of travellers.
Cathay is among four carriers operating direct flights between Hong Kong and the Middle East.
Emirates, Qatar and Etihad Airways earlier announced on their websites limited services between Hong Kong and Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi, respectively.
Experts earlier told the South China Morning Post that the impact of Cathay halting services between the city and the Middle East would be limited. The drop in demand and the fact that most flights were operated by Middle Eastern carriers meant the city’s airline industry would not be hugely affected, they said.
The ongoing Middle East conflict also prompted a surge in fuel surcharges on plane tickets this month, with Cathay and its budget arm HK Express doubling the fees last week.
Greater Bay Airlines also raised its fuel surcharges by more than double, and Hong Kong Airlines raised its fees by up to 35.2 per cent.

data.title: Rights to antibody drug from China’s Keymed change hands in US acquisition
data.description:
US biopharmaceutical company Gilead Sciences agreed to buy Ouro Medicines, which is developing an antibody-based medicine for autoimmune diseases licensed from China’s Keymed Biosciences, in a deal worth up to US$2.18 billion.
Gilead Sciences is among a group of global drug makers facing patent expirations and turning to mergers, acquisitions and licensing deals to refill their drug pipelines.
Under the agreement, Gilead Sciences would pay shareholders of US-based Ouro Medicines US$1.675 billion in cash upfront, plus up to US$500 million tied to milestone payments, according to an exchange filing on Tuesday.
The transaction aimed to speed up the global development of CM336, or OM336, an experimental treatment that could be the best-in-class T-cell-engaging antibody for autoimmune diseases, the filing said.
Ouro holds exclusive rights – licensed from Sichuan-based Keymed in 2024 – to develop, manufacture and commercialise CM336 outside mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan. In return, Keymed received upfront and near-term payments and a minority equity interest in Ouro, which now stood at about 15 per cent, according to a separate filing with the Hong Kong stock exchange.
On completion of the acquisition by Gilead, Keymed expects to receive about US$250 million in upfront cash, plus up to US$70 million in additional milestone payments, for a total potential payout of about US$320 million.
Keymed’s Hong Kong-traded shares gained 6.5 per cent on the day to HK$53.75 on Tuesday.
Global spending on life sciences mergers and acquisitions jumped 81 per cent in 2025 to US$240 billion, as global pharmaceutical giants turned to deals to plug an expected growth gap of up to US$370 billion by 2032 owing to blockbuster drugs losing patent protection, according to consulting firm EY.
This year has already yielded a string of deals. On Monday, Abbott Laboratories said it expected to close its US$21 billion acquisition of cancer diagnostics company Exact Sciences after receiving all required regulatory clearances.
On March 6, French drug maker Servier agreed to pay US$2.5 billion in cash for the US oncology biotech firm Day One Biopharmaceuticals, representing a premium of about 68 per cent to its last traded price, according to Servier.
Early stage drugs developed by Chinese pharmaceutical firms have become a hunting ground for global companies looking for fresh products – and the pace of deals is increasing. Licensing deals by Chinese drug firms this year totalled about US$52 billion from 41 transactions as of February 24, according to JPMorgan, following a record 157 deals worth a combined US$135.7 billion last year.
One of the latest is Sino Biopharmaceutical’s deal with Sanofi in early March, worth up to US$1.53 billion, which grants the French drug maker exclusive global rights outside China to develop, manufacture and sell an oral drug for blood cancer and immune-related diseases.

data.title: HKIC, Gobi Partners and HKU launch fund to back university research start-ups
data.description:
Hong Kong Investment Corporation (HKIC), the government’s investment arm, has teamed up with venture capital firm Gobi Partners and the University of Hong Kong (HKU) to launch a fund on Tuesday to invest in technology start-ups spun off from the university’s research breakthroughs, according to an HKIC statement.
Gobi-HKU Fund I made two investments at its launch: Manifold Tech, which develops spatial intelligence for robotics, and AilsynBio, which focuses on artificial intelligence-driven drug design and recovery.
“The launch of Gobi-HKU Fund I, along with its swift and substantive deployment of capital, demonstrates the HKIC’s steadfast determination to foster synergy among the core sectors of innovation and technology – namely investment, industry, academia, research and government,” said HKIC CEO Clara Chan Ka-chai.
Chan told the South China Morning Post that the fund used a milestone approach, meaning it would invest a small amount in individual projects initially. When projects met predefined targets such as commercialisation, HKIC and partners could quickly top up from the pool of capital earmarked in the fund.
Backed by the government’s reserves, HKIC was established in 2022 to boost the city’s economy and innovation industry. It managed HK$62 billion (US$8 billion) in government funds and had invested in more than 190 projects as of February.

Besides direct investment, HKIC has also brought together start-ups and established companies to collaborate through networking activities. Last week, it announced the establishment of the Hong Kong RISC-V Alliance.
The newly launched fund was one of the mandates under the Patient Capital Strategic Fund, set up by HKIC and Gobi Partners in May. The partnership leveraged Gobi Partners’ expertise to identify projects and make investments promptly, Chan said.
Gobi Partners, based in Hong Kong and Kuala Lumpur with US$2 billion of assets under management, invests in sectors including AI, fintech, consumer, healthcare and sustainability.
“Some of the world’s most disruptive technologies are being built in the halls of Hong Kong’s universities,” said Gobi Partners managing partner Chibo Tang. He said the partnership with HKU would help “bridge the gap between research and commercialisation, bringing breakthroughs in AI, robotics, biotech and other critical sectors to the world”.
The new fund is also backed by HKU’s Entrepreneurship Engine Fund, which provides capital to support HKU spin-offs in their development and long-term growth.
“Our partnership with Gobi Partners strengthens HKU’s mission to turn breakthrough research into real-world solutions,” said Stephanie Ma, vice-president and pro-vice-chancellor (research) of HKU. “With new investments already under way and more support on the horizon, we are accelerating the growth of high-potential university start-ups. This is just the beginning of HKU’s expanding innovation journey.”

data.title: Hong Kong’s ESF schools group plans to increase fees by 4.1% on average next year
data.description:
Hong Kong’s largest international school group plans to raise tuition fees by an average of 4.1 per cent for most of its primary and secondary divisions next academic year, pending approval by authorities.
Parents with children enrolled in kindergartens operated by the English Schools Foundation (ESF) may face a higher fee rise, with a proposed increase of 5.8 per cent.
At Discovery College in Discovery Bay on Lantau Island – one of ESF’s private independent schools – parents of secondary students would pay HK$1,030 (US$131) more per month, the biggest rise among all ESF schools in terms of the actual amount.
Belinda Greer, CEO of the ESF, said in a letter to parents that the average 4.1 per cent increase would allow the foundation to maintain teaching quality.
“To continue investing in our schools, our growing technology needs, and – most importantly – the exceptional teachers who inspire your children every day, the board has approved an average fee increase of 4.1 per cent,” she said.
Compared with the past two years, the proposed fee hike is smaller. The ESF applied for increases of 4.8 per cent for the 2025 school year and 5 per cent for 2024, both of which were eventually approved by authorities.
Kindergarten fees would range from HK$107,200 to HK$126,600 in the coming school year. Parents whose children are enrolled in the bilingual stream at Abacus Kindergarten in Sai Kung would pay HK$690 more per month.

For most ESF primary and secondary international schools, parents would pay HK$600 to HK$720 more per month, equating to annual fees of HK$145,000 for primary and HK$188,300 for secondary if approved.
Discovery College, one of ESF’s two private independent schools and the most expensive among them, will see fees rise by about 4.8 per cent, meaning parents will pay HK$780 more per month in the primary section and HK$1,030 more in the secondary section.
Renaissance College in Ma On Shan, the other private independent school operated by the ESF, would raise fees by 4.1 per cent in the next school year, to HK$154,200 for primary and between HK$201,500 and HK$203,700 for secondary levels.
Unlike international schools, which mainly serve non-local students, private independent schools in Hong Kong primarily meet demand from local families seeking alternatives to public sector institutions and must ensure that at least 70 per cent of their students are Hong Kong permanent residents.
Greer said the ESF’s fees remain “one of the most affordable”.
“We know we are living in uncertain times, and we are mindful of the economic pressures many families face. Please be assured that our commitment to providing ‘best value’ remains our top priority. By managing our costs carefully, we are proud to remain one of the most affordable international school groups in Hong Kong,” she said.

data.title: Hong Kong’s eMPF to cut fees from April, saving US$6.4 billion in 10 years
data.description:
The electronic platform of the Mandatory Provident Fund (eMPF) will cut administration fees for 378 investment funds by more than 21 per cent from April 1, the pension regulator said on Tuesday, adding that this will achieve its cost-saving target of HK$50 billion (US$6.4 billion) in less than 10 years.
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po approved the reduction, which would lower administration costs from 0.37 per cent to 0.29 per cent of assets under management, representing a 21.6 per cent fee cut for all 4.8 million MPF scheme members, the Mandatory Provident Fund Schemes Authority (MPFA) said in a statement.
MPFA chairwoman Ayesha Macpherson Lau said the fee cut would deliver higher savings for members.
“The original estimate of HK$30 billion to HK$40 billion in administrative cost savings over 10 years of operation [of the eMPF] has now been increased to HK$50 billion and accelerated to less than 10 years,” Lau said, reiterating the revised forecast she made in December.
Rolled out in June 2024, the eMPF serves as a centralised platform replacing the separate systems used by the MPF’s 12 trustees. It allows trustees, as well as the city’s 350,000 employers and 4.8 million members, to manage HK$1.63 trillion in assets through a single system accessible via smartphones, tablets and desktop computers.

Digitisation has already driven down costs. The administration rate fell from 0.58 per cent before the platform’s launch to 0.37 per cent today. The further reduction reflects fees charged by the eMPF at half the rate previously levied by the trustees.
“The digital usage rate of eMPF in the past 22 months has exceeded expectations,” Lau said. “Together with economies of scale and the growth in MPF assets, this has not only realised the policy intent of launching the eMPF, but also surpassed the initially projected level and pace of fee reduction.”
Initially, the MPFA expected it would take 10 years after the eMPF was fully implemented to reduce administration fees from 0.58 per cent to 0.2 to 0.25 per cent, but Lau said in December that the target would be reached within five years.
Strong investment gains and contributions lifted total MPF assets to an all-time high of HK$1.63 trillion in the first two months of 2026, averaging HK$340,216 per member, according to MPF Ratings, an independent consultancy that tracks the scheme’s performance. The 378 investment funds recorded HK$71.1 billion in profits during the period, after gaining HK$260 billion in 2025 as a whole.
However, MPF Ratings chairman Francis Chung warned of volatility ahead, citing the US-Israel war against Iran that began at the end of last month. The Hang Seng Index fell 7 per cent over the past three weeks and is down 3 per cent this year.

data.title: Hong Kong’s eMPF to cut fees from April, saving US$6.4 billion in 10 years
data.description:
The electronic platform of the Mandatory Provident Fund (eMPF) will cut administration fees for 378 investment funds by more than 21 per cent from April 1, the pension regulator said on Tuesday, adding that this will achieve its cost-saving target of HK$50 billion (US$6.4 billion) in less than 10 years.
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po approved the reduction, which would lower administration costs from 0.37 per cent to 0.29 per cent of assets under management, representing a 21.6 per cent fee cut for all 4.8 million MPF scheme members, the Mandatory Provident Fund Schemes Authority (MPFA) said in a statement.
MPFA chairwoman Ayesha Macpherson Lau said the fee cut would deliver higher savings for members.
“The original estimate of HK$30 billion to HK$40 billion in administrative cost savings over 10 years of operation [of the eMPF] has now been increased to HK$50 billion and accelerated to less than 10 years,” Lau said, reiterating the revised forecast she made in December.
Rolled out in June 2024, the eMPF serves as a centralised platform replacing the separate systems used by the MPF’s 12 trustees. It allows trustees, as well as the city’s 350,000 employers and 4.8 million members, to manage HK$1.63 trillion in assets through a single system accessible via smartphones, tablets and desktop computers.

Digitisation has already driven down costs. The administration rate fell from 0.58 per cent before the platform’s launch to 0.37 per cent today. The further reduction reflects fees charged by the eMPF at half the rate previously levied by the trustees.
“The digital usage rate of eMPF in the past 22 months has exceeded expectations,” Lau said. “Together with economies of scale and the growth in MPF assets, this has not only realised the policy intent of launching the eMPF, but also surpassed the initially projected level and pace of fee reduction.”
Initially, the MPFA expected it would take 10 years after the eMPF was fully implemented to reduce administration fees from 0.58 per cent to 0.2 to 0.25 per cent, but Lau said in December that the target would be reached within five years.
Strong investment gains and contributions lifted total MPF assets to an all-time high of HK$1.63 trillion in the first two months of 2026, averaging HK$340,216 per member, according to MPF Ratings, an independent consultancy that tracks the scheme’s performance. The 378 investment funds recorded HK$71.1 billion in profits during the period, after gaining HK$260 billion in 2025 as a whole.
However, MPF Ratings chairman Francis Chung warned of volatility ahead, citing the US-Israel war against Iran that began at the end of last month. The Hang Seng Index fell 7 per cent over the past three weeks and is down 3 per cent this year.

data.title: Government to launch guide to help elderly Hongkongers with revised HK$2 fares
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities will launch advertisments and a transport guide to help elderly residents adapt to the revised HK$2 (26 US cents) transport fare scheme coming into effect next Friday, according to the city’s welfare chief.
Secretary for Labour and Welfare Chris Sun Yuk-han on Tuesday said the government would publicise changes to the scheme extensively and roll out a guide covering different districts in the coming days.
“On Monday, I met with district councillors and care teams from different districts and they offered many suggestions, such as how to make it easier for the elderly to differentiate routes with the HK$2 fare and those with a 20 per cent discount,” he told a radio show.
Posters and explanatory materials would be displayed at government-run elderly centres while ambassadors for the scheme would be stationed at 75 locations across Hong Kong, he said.
Citing Yuen Long as an example, Sun noted that many elderly people were used to taking long-haul buses from the New Territories into town despite the short journey.
“Our guide can teach them how to take short-haul buses that cost below HK$10, and avoid routes beginning with the number 9, which are usually urban routes that cross the harbour,” he said.
An example in the guide showed that elderly commuters could get from Yee Wo Street in Causeway Bay to Queen Street in Sheung Wan by taking bus 5B, bus 26 or the tram, instead of bus 969, which runs between Causeway Bay and Tin Shui Wai. This would save them HK$3.50.
Sun said that the Transport Department’s Traffic Control Centre in Kai Tak would be activated on April 3, the first day of the new “HK$2 flat rate or 80 per cent discount” model, to monitor the situation and respond promptly to any problems that arose.
He revealed that a major system reconfiguration had been carried out over the past year, involving the review of more than 17,000 Octopus cards, and all testing work had been completed.
Sun said that the scheme currently benefited 2.67 million Hongkongers, but as the city’s population aged, the number of people receiving subsidies would increase.
He added that the government needed to maintain the policy’s fiscal sustainability and that the new measures could significantly address the issue of elderly passengers taking long-haul buses for a short distance.

For seniors who needed to take long-distance transport, Sun stressed that the government still covered 80 per cent of the fares and the subsidy amount remained substantial.
He said the size of the subsidy must be weighed against future population ageing and long-term fiscal commitments to ensure the scheme could sustainably serve more people in the future.
Regarding the monthly cap of 240 trips, which will not take effect until next year, Sun said that since the beneficiaries account for about a third of Hong Kong’s population, the government would implement the changes “one step at a time”.
“Careful consideration must be given to their need for time to adapt to the change, and the next step is to address the fare limit,” he said.
Sun said that after a re-evaluation of the scheme, the government now projected it would save HK$550 million in the next financial year, down from the HK$680 million previously estimated in last year’s budget.

data.title: Surging demand for AI agents fuels Hong Kong’s race for computing power
data.description:
As leading Chinese chip executives predict that autonomous AI agents will trigger an unprecedented explosion in computing demand, Hong Kong is accelerating efforts to expand its artificial intelligence computing capacity.
Calling token consumption in the era of AI “far beyond our imagination”, Zhang Jianzhong, founder and CEO of graphics processing unit designer Moore Threads, said at an AI summit in Hong Kong on Saturday that the exponential surge in demand had made it impossible for anyone to accurately predict future needs.
Zhang argued that competitive edge was no longer about sheer volume; rather, it was to do with cost efficiency. “Whoever offers the cheapest tokens will be able to build better infrastructure for everyone,” he said.
Chen Weiliang, chairman and general manager of chip designer MetaX, echoed Zhang’s views.
Citing a recent SemiAnalysis report, Chen attributed a 6,400 per cent increase in average daily token consumption to the industry’s transition from basic large language models to agentic AI workflows.

Hong Kong currently has 5 ExaFLOPS of computing power, compared with 60 ExaFLOPS in Beijing and 120 ExaFLOPS in Shanghai, according to Zhang, another speaker at the AI Summit.
The current Hong Kong administration has made significant strides in building up its AI infrastructure, according to Peter Yan King-shun, director general of the government’s Office for Attracting Strategic Enterprises.
Yan noted the recently announced supercomputing hub in the Northern Metropolis, which was projected to boost the city’s total computing power 36-fold to 180 ExaFLOPS over its first three years.
Hong Kong’s local expansion is unfolding against the backdrop of a massive nationwide push on the Chinese mainland. The country’s total computing power hit 962 ExaFLOPS at the end of June 2025, following a 73 per cent year-on-year surge, according to a report by the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology.
China now accounts for 21 per cent of the world’s total computing capacity. The report also noted that over the past eight years the country maintained an average annual growth rate of 48 per cent in computing power, outpacing global averages.
Forward-looking investments are also fuelled by the anticipation of future growth. Futurist Rohit Talwar, who defines artificial general intelligence (AGI) as AI operating at human parity across all intellectual domains, said that organisations were currently working on securing the right infrastructure, talent, data and platforms to ensure they are ready for AGI when it arrives.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks rebound on hopes of a US-Iran deal as tensions ease
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks rebounded on Tuesday on signs of a de-escalation of the US-Israel war on Iran.
The Hang Seng Index rose 1.2 per cent to 24,678.33 as of 9.42am local time. The Hang Seng Tech Index gained 0.3 per cent. On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index climbed 0.1 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index retreated 0.4 per cent.
Laopu Gold jumped 11 per cent to HK$619.50 and machine tool maker Techtronic Industries surged 9.4 per cent to HK$112.20. Alibaba Group Holding added 1.1 per cent to HK$121 and Tencent Holdings rose 1.4 per cent to HK$505.50.
US President Donald Trump said on Monday that he was withholding assaults on Iranian energy infrastructure for five days, citing “major points of agreement” with Iran. He said special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner had held discussions on Sunday evening with a “top person” on the Iranian side, claiming both parties were keen to strike a deal and would talk again by phone.
Two companies made their debut in Hong Kong. Zhejiang Galaxis Technology Group soared 81 per cent to HK$30.14 and Jiangsu New Vision Automotive Electronics rose 1.3 per cent to HK$44.76.
Other major Asian markets all edged higher. Japan’s Nikkei 225 climbed 0.9 per cent, while South Korea’s Kospi rose 1.5 per cent and Australia’s S&P/ASX200 added 0.4 per cent.

data.title: Hong Kong proposes heavy penalties for smoking on construction sites
data.description:
Contractors could face fines of up to HK$400,000 (US$51,068) for failing to prevent smoking at construction sites in Hong Kong under a government proposal to improve safety following the deadly Tai Po fire last November.
The proposals, outlined in a paper prepared by the Labour Bureau, aim to tighten existing workplace safety laws and impose a strict blanket smoking ban on all construction sites.
Construction workers could face a HK$3,000 fine for smoking on-site, while contractors would also be held responsible for failing to prevent smoking and could be fined up to HK$400,000.
The government will also consider invoking the general duty provisions of the Factories and Industrial Undertakings Ordinance to prosecute proprietors or employees involved in serious cases, according to the paper.
In such cases presenting a catastrophic fire risk – for example, a worker smoking near highly flammable materials – the worker would be liable to a HK$150,000 fine and six months’ imprisonment, while the proprietor could face a HK$3 million fine and the same jail term.
The paper, which will be presented at the Legislative Council’s manpower panel meeting next Monday, states that the smoking ban will apply to all areas of construction sites, whether outdoors or indoors.

According to the bureau, the HK$3,000 fixed penalty is intended to align with the existing Fixed Penalty (Smoking Offences) Ordinance.
“The proposed legislative amendments could mitigate the fire risk associated with smoking in construction sites and protect persons in construction sites from exposure to second-hand smoke, thereby enhancing the safety and health of workers in construction sites,” the paper said.
However, it adds that “occupied domestic premises and occupied private quarters by residents” will be regarded as “exempt areas”. Otherwise, “all indoor and outdoor areas of a construction site will be specified as a no-smoking area,”.
The government aims to introduce the legislative amendments to Legco by the middle of this year.
The proposed changes form part of a wider package of safety reforms at construction sites following the deadly fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, which claimed 168 lives and displaced around 5,000 people.
Residents reported that during the major renovation project at the housing complex, workers were repeatedly seen smoking on-site.
A public inquiry last week also heard that the fire was “most likely” caused by someone lighting a cigarette, which ignited flammable materials stacked in the lightwell.

data.title: Residents to testify as hearing into deadly Tai Po fire enters third day
data.description:
In his opening speech, Dawes said six “human factors” led to the almost complete failure of fire safety measures at the housing estate before it was engulfed in the 43-hour blaze that started in the afternoon of November 26, last year.
The use of combustible scaffolding mesh, the fire alarm system’s deactivation, removal of fireproof windows from emergency passages and hosepipes being turned off for months were likely to have contributed to the tragedy, the committee heard.
Other failures included the use of polyfoam boards to block windows and workers’ smoking habits, an issue that residents had repeatedly raised but was ignored.
The blaze was likely to have been caused by cigarettes burning flammable materials in a light well, Dawes said, adding that the deadliest fire in Hong Kong in seven decades had revealed unacceptable systemic failings which could not be overlooked.

data.title: Hong Kong’s national security better protected with changes, experts say
data.description:
Changes to Hong Kong’s implementation rules for the national security law will improve operational efficiency amid heightened geopolitical risks, experts have said.
A legal expert and a former security minister said the amendments aimed to clearly lay out investigation procedures for searches under existing national security law powers, while an academic argued the move was needed to address online threats that could involve external forces.
“There are new procedures, but there are no new powers or principles,” Senior Counsel Ronny Tong Ka-wah, who sits on the key decision-making Executive Council, said.
The government gazetted a series of amendments to the implementation rules of the Beijing-imposed national security law on Monday. A spokesman said the changes would strengthen enforcement power, improve investigation of cases and mitigate national security risks more promptly.
The changes include empowering police to require a person under investigation to provide passwords or decryption methods for electronic equipment, with non-compliance punishable by a maximum of one year’s imprisonment and a HK$100,000 (US$12,760) fine.
Customs officers were also empowered to seize articles deemed seditious, and authorities said they would set up applicable procedures and a strict timetable for processing claims of legal professional privilege involving materials seized by police for investigation.
Penalties for several existing offences were also increased.
Overseas political organisations or agents that fail to provide information to authorities can now face a maximum of one year’s imprisonment, up from six months.
Those providing false or misleading information can face up to three years’ imprisonment, compared with the previous two.
Tong said authorising police officers to demand passwords or decryption for electronic devices was in line with officers’ existing power to enter a suspect’s residence when equipped with a warrant in today’s digital age.
“If [officers] can enter someone’s home, why can’t they enter their phones? That’s included, but it’s not stipulated in the law,” Tong said.
“This will still require a court warrant with reasonable cause.”
While Tong agreed the city’s criminal law did not stipulate the power for officers to obtain unencrypted access to electronic devices, the barrister noted such ability was already granted overseas, such as in the United Kingdom.
Tong added that setting up dedicated mechanisms for handling materials that could involve legal professional privilege would only increase certainty for lawyers and defendants on what to expect during an investigation.

Giving customs the power to seize seditious articles was also in line with the obligation of all government departments to enforce the national security law, he said.
Regina Ip Lau Suk-yee, Exco convenor and a former security chief, said the amendments improved enforcement powers in light of practical experience and court judgments, particularly the new rules compelling the provision of passwords for electronic devices.
Ip noted that police currently lacked the legal authority to require individuals under investigation to unlock their devices. She said the process of accessing the devices could be time-consuming, despite authorities’ ability to do so, and the revised rules would speed up investigations.
“They need the powers to require the owner of the device to provide a password or decryption assistance in the interest of time,” she said. “Time is of the essence in such investigations.”
While the extensive amendments did not require legislative approval, Ip dismissed concerns the process lacked checks and balances, emphasising the power to formulate the rules rested with the chief executive and the Committee for Safeguarding National Security.
The changes had undergone careful consideration, she added.
“National security laws are in a special category. If you want to improve the effectiveness of the investigation, you do not consult,” Ip said, describing the process as consulting “thieves” when trying to catch them.
One of the major amendments came after the Court of Final Appeal last year quashed the convictions of three core members of the now-disbanded Hong Kong Alliance in Support of Patriotic Democratic Movements of China, who had been jailed for failing to help with a police investigation.
Ip said it was legitimate under the common law system for a government to introduce amendments to plug a loophole after losing a case, describing it as a “fairly standard procedure”.
Lau Siu-kai, a consultant to the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said the amendments followed the conclusion of several landmark national security cases, including that of former Apple Daily owner Jimmy Lai Chee-ying and the subversion trial of 47 activists who took part in the unofficial 2020 primary election.
He said the changes were also related to the ongoing US-China rivalry, highlighting new rules targeting the dissemination of electronic messages that endangered national security, which he said often involved external forces.
“This amendment should strengthen the government’s capacity to safeguard national security and deter both domestic and foreign adversaries,” he said.
Lau added that authorities must continue addressing threats to national security on electronic channels, an increasingly serious concern.
